frameworks.name: Granger causal regression
frameworks.one_line: lag-based predictive causality regression benchmarked on BOLD time series.

frameworks.name: Group Iterative Multiple Model Estimation (GIMME)
frameworks.one_line: group-level SEM-based effective connectivity recovery from fMRI.

frameworks.name: non-Gaussian independent-component causal discovery
frameworks.one_line: exploit non-Gaussianity of BOLD to identify directed feedback structure.

frameworks.name: Fast Adjacency Skewness (FASK) and Two-Step
frameworks.one_line: two new non-Gaussian algorithms introduced and theoretically justified.

core_question: Which statistical methods reliably recover feedforward and feedback effective connections, including cyclic structure, from BOLD fMRI time series?
key_assumptions: BOLD signals carry non-Gaussian features informative for causal direction
effective connectivity can include direct feedback (2-cycles) and excitatory/inhibitory loops
synthetic ground-truth networks built on macaque anatomy are valid testbeds
manipulations: method choice across nine causal-discovery algorithms
presence vs absence of direct feedback (2-cycles) in ground-truth networks
synthetic macaque-derived networks vs empirical human resting-state and task fMRI
key_findings: compared adequacy of multiple existing methods on synthetic BOLD with known feedback structure
introduced FASK and Two-Step and provided theoretical justification for both
evaluated methods on macaque-anatomy-based models and on empirical human resting-state and task data
interpretations: non-Gaussian features of BOLD can be leveraged to recover cyclic effective connectivity from fMRI
the new FASK and Two-Step algorithms are positioned as improved tools for feedback connectivity inference
